_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Forsyth County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Forsyth County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Winston-Salem, NC — 200 N. Main Street (Hall of Justice; county offices and courts across multiple downtown buildings)

previous_courthouses.location: Winston, NC (separate from Salem; the two cities merged in 1913)
previous_courthouses.fate: First courthouse built when Forsyth County was formed from Stokes County in 1849. Replaced when outgrown.
Second courthouse served until replaced by the 1926 Hall of Justice. No fire or war damage.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or war damage. County formed in 1849 from Stokes County.
record_losses.description: Forsyth County was formed from Stokes County in 1849 and has suffered no major courthouse fire or war destruction. Records begin at formation and run continuously. For pre-1849 records, Stokes County (Danbury) is the parent county. But the true genealogical jewel of Forsyth County is not the courthouse at all — it is the Moravian Archives in Old Salem, which hold one of the most complete colonial-era record collections in North America.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The courthouse records are solid but secondary to the Moravian Archives for early Forsyth County research. If {ancestor_name} had any connection to the Moravian settlement at Salem, the Archives may hold more detailed records of {pronoun_possessive} life than any civil source in the state.
record_losses.source: Fries, Adelaide L., et al. Records of the Moravians in North Carolina. 13 vols. Raleigh: NC Historical Commission, 1922-2006.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1849 deeds for land within current Forsyth County boundaries are in Stokes County records at Danbury.
deeds.repository: Forsyth County Register of Deeds, Winston-Salem; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from formation
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1849 wills for this area are in Stokes County records.
wills_estates.repository: Forsyth County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: Pre-1849 marriages are in Stokes County records. Moravian marriages are separately recorded in church archives.
marriages.repository: Forsyth County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Forsyth County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Winston-Salem city health department records may predate 1913.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: The Moravian Archives in Old Salem, Winston-Salem, hold one of the most complete colonial-era record collections in America. The Moravians were meticulous record keepers. The Archives contain: church diaries (daily accounts of life in Salem and its satellite communities); individual biographies (Lebensläufe) written at death for every congregation member; baptismal, marriage, and burial registers; detailed maps of town lots and surrounding farmland; commercial account books documenting every financial transaction in the Moravian economy; correspondence with Moravian headquarters in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, and Herrnhut, Germany. These records begin in the 1750s — nearly a century before Forsyth County was formed.
substitute_sources.repository: Moravian Archives, 457 S. Church Street, Winston-Salem; published in Fries et al., Records of the Moravians in North Carolina (13 vols.); microfilm at NC State Archives; selected digitization online
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Among the most valuable genealogical sources in the southeastern United States. For any family with a Moravian connection, the Archives provide life narratives, family relationships, daily activities, commercial dealings, and spiritual life in detail that no other colonial-era source in North Carolina approaches. The Lebensläufe (life accounts) are functionally biographical sketches written about each congregant — if {ancestor_name} was Moravian, there may be a written account of {pronoun_possessive} entire life.
substitute_sources.source: Fries, Adelaide L., et al. Records of the Moravians in North Carolina. 13 vols. Raleigh: NC Historical Commission, 1922-2006. Sensbach, Jon F. A Separate Canaan: The Making of an Afro-Moravian World in North Carolina, 1763-1840. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for the territory that became Forsyth County are found in Stokes County (Danbury) before 1849. Stokes County was formed in 1789 and has generally good record survival.
substitute_sources.repository: Stokes County Register of Deeds, Danbury; NC State Archives microfilm; FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Forsyth County family research before 1849. Non-Moravian families in particular — those living outside the Salem congregation's orbit — will only appear in Stokes County civil records for the pre-1849 period.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Reynolds was Winston-Salem's dominant employer from the 1880s through the mid-twentieth century. Company employment records, payroll ledgers, and some personnel files survive. These are particularly valuable for documenting Black workers in the stemming houses — a workforce that is often invisible in other record types.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; Wake Forest University Z. Smith Reynolds Library Special Collections; some records remain proprietary
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying tobacco factory workers and tracking their employment. For Black workers especially, Reynolds company records may be the most specific documentation available, since many Black tobacco workers did not own property and left minimal traces in courthouse records.
substitute_sources.source: Korstad, Robert Rodgers. Civil Rights Unionism: Tobacco Workers and the Struggle for Democracy in the Mid-Twentieth-Century South. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2003.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Before 1850, families in present-day Forsyth County territory were enumerated under Stokes County. From 1850 onward, under Forsyth County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families across the 1849 county formation boundary.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Forsyth and Stokes Counties, NC.

narrative_hooks: If {ancestor_name} had any connection to the Moravian community at Salem, the Moravian Archives may hold a Lebenslauf — a written biography composed at death — that tells {pronoun_possessive} life story in the congregants' own words. No other colonial-era source in North Carolina comes close to this level of personal detail.

The Moravian Archives in Old Salem contain church diaries, maps, account books, and personal biographies dating back to the 1750s — nearly a century before Forsyth County even existed. If {ancestor_name}'s family intersected with the Moravian settlement, the trail may lead here before it leads to any courthouse.

If {ancestor_name} worked at the R.J. Reynolds tobacco factory in Winston-Salem, the company employment records may be the best source for documenting {pronoun_possessive} time in the city — especially for Black stemming house workers who left few traces in other record types.

Forsyth County was formed from Stokes County in 1849, so pre-1849 records lead to the Stokes County courthouse at Danbury — unless the family was Moravian, in which case the trail leads to Old Salem, which kept its own records independently of any county government.
source_references: Fries, Adelaide L., et al. Records of the Moravians in North Carolina. 13 vols. Raleigh: NC Historical Commission, 1922-2006.
Sensbach, Jon F. A Separate Canaan: The Making of an Afro-Moravian World in North Carolina, 1763-1840. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.
Korstad, Robert Rodgers. Civil Rights Unionism: Tobacco Workers and the Struggle for Democracy in the Mid-Twentieth-Century South. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2003.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: North Carolina County Records. Raleigh, 1937–1942.
